prompt: Which method of authorizing access to a storage account is the most secure?
choices: Microsoft Entra ID with Azure RBAC data roles
The account's shared access keys
A shared access signature (SAS)
Anonymous public read access
correctAnswers: Microsoft Entra ID with Azure RBAC data roles
explanation: Entra + RBAC is identity-based with no keys to manage — the most secure option.

prompt: What is a shared access signature (SAS)?
choices: A scoped, time-limited, revocable token granting specific rights
A permanent account-level password
A certificate used only for TLS
A managed identity for the storage account
correctAnswers: A scoped, time-limited, revocable token granting specific rights
explanation: A SAS grants narrow, time-bound access without sharing the full account key.

prompt: Rank the storage authorization methods from most to least secure.
choices: Entra ID + RBAC > user-delegation SAS > account-key SAS > shared account key
Shared account key > account-key SAS > user-delegation SAS > Entra ID + RBAC
Account-key SAS > Entra ID + RBAC > shared account key > user-delegation SAS
All four are equally secure
correctAnswers: Entra ID + RBAC > user-delegation SAS > account-key SAS > shared account key
explanation: Identity-based is strongest; a user-delegation SAS (signed with Entra creds) beats an account-key SAS, which beats the raw shared key.

prompt: You must give a partner time-limited read access to a single blob without sharing the account keys. What should you issue?
choices: A user-delegation SAS scoped to that blob
The primary account access key
A Reader RBAC role at the subscription
A managed identity for the partner
correctAnswers: A user-delegation SAS scoped to that blob
explanation: A SAS (ideally user-delegation, signed with Entra credentials) grants scoped, time-bound access to specific resources without exposing account keys.

prompt: What is the strongest way to prevent shared-key access to a storage account entirely?
choices: Disable shared-key authorization so only Entra-based access works
Rotate the keys weekly
Store the keys in Key Vault
Set a resource lock on the account
correctAnswers: Disable shared-key authorization so only Entra-based access works
explanation: Disabling shared-key access forces all requests through Microsoft Entra authorization. Rotation and vaulting reduce but don't eliminate key use.

prompt: Which identity sources can be used for Azure Files identity-based authentication over SMB?
choices: On-premises AD DS, Microsoft Entra Domain Services, or Microsoft Entra Kerberos
Only on-premises AD DS
Only app registrations
Only local accounts on each client
correctAnswers: On-premises AD DS, Microsoft Entra Domain Services, or Microsoft Entra Kerberos
explanation: Azure Files supports exactly these three identity sources for Kerberos-based SMB authentication.

prompt: How many identity sources for Azure Files identity-based authentication can be enabled on ONE storage account?
choices: Exactly one
Two — one for SMB and one for NFS
Unlimited, mixed per share
Zero — Azure Files doesn't support identity-based auth
correctAnswers: Exactly one
explanation: Only one identity source can be enabled per storage account; you can't mix AD DS and Entra Kerberos on the same account.

prompt: After share-level access is granted on an Azure Files share, what provides fine-grained control over individual files and folders?
choices: Windows ACLs / NTFS permissions
SAS tokens per file
Per-file Azure RBAC data roles
Blob versioning
correctAnswers: Windows ACLs / NTFS permissions
explanation: Once the share is accessible, standard NTFS ACLs on files/folders apply, just like an on-premises SMB share.

prompt: Which Azure Files identity source requires NO on-premises domain controller?
choices: Microsoft Entra Kerberos
On-premises AD DS
A gMSA on a member server
A read-only domain controller in Azure
correctAnswers: Microsoft Entra Kerberos
explanation: Microsoft Entra Kerberos is the cloud-only option, letting Entra users access shares without a domain controller.

prompt: What does soft delete protect against in Azure Storage?
choices: Accidental or malicious deletion, recoverable within a retention window
Unauthorized read access to data
Weak TLS negotiation
Slow performance under load
correctAnswers: Accidental or malicious deletion, recoverable within a retention window
explanation: Soft delete lets you recover deleted blobs/containers/file shares within a configured retention period.

prompt: What does blob versioning provide?
choices: A history of previous versions of a blob after it is overwritten
Encryption of blob contents
Automatic geo-replication
Access control per blob version only
correctAnswers: A history of previous versions of a blob after it is overwritten
explanation: Versioning preserves prior states of a blob whenever it is modified or overwritten.

prompt: What distinguishes immutable storage (WORM) from soft delete and versioning?
choices: It actively prevents modification/deletion during the retention period, even by an admin
It only recovers deleted data, like soft delete
It only preserves overwrite history, like versioning
It is a form of encryption
correctAnswers: It actively prevents modification/deletion during the retention period, even by an admin
explanation: Immutable storage enforces WORM (time-based or legal hold), blocking change/deletion regardless of privilege — unlike the recover-after-the-fact features.

prompt: A compliance mandate requires that financial records cannot be altered or deleted for 7 years, even by an owner. Which feature meets this?
choices: A time-based immutability (WORM) policy, set to Locked
Soft delete with a 7-year retention
Blob versioning
A read-only resource lock on the storage account
correctAnswers: A time-based immutability (WORM) policy, set to Locked
explanation: Only a locked WORM policy guarantees data cannot be modified or deleted during the retention period, even by privileged users.

prompt: What does enabling 'Secure transfer required' on a storage account enforce?
choices: All connections must use HTTPS
A client certificate on every request
Customer-managed keys
Soft delete
correctAnswers: All connections must use HTTPS
explanation: Secure transfer required forces encrypted transport (HTTPS) for all requests.

prompt: What does Bring Your Own Key (BYOK) mean for Azure Storage encryption?
choices: You supply a customer-managed key stored in Key Vault instead of the Microsoft-managed key
You manage encryption entirely outside Azure
You disable encryption at rest
You share the storage account key with clients
correctAnswers: You supply a customer-managed key stored in Key Vault instead of the Microsoft-managed key
explanation: BYOK gives you control of the encryption key via Key Vault; encryption at rest remains on regardless.

prompt: What does infrastructure (double) encryption add on top of the standard service-level encryption?
choices: A second, independent layer of encryption at the platform level
A faster encryption algorithm
Replacement of Microsoft keys with customer keys
Encryption only for data in transit
correctAnswers: A second, independent layer of encryption at the platform level
explanation: Double encryption stacks a second independent encryption layer for the highest-confidentiality needs.

prompt: How do BYOK (customer-managed keys) and infrastructure double encryption differ?
choices: BYOK is about WHO controls the key; double encryption is about HOW MANY independent layers apply
They are the same feature
BYOK adds a second layer; double encryption changes the key owner
Both only apply to data in transit
correctAnswers: BYOK is about WHO controls the key; double encryption is about HOW MANY independent layers apply
explanation: They are orthogonal and stackable: key ownership vs number of encryption layers.

prompt: Which access-control layer does Azure Blob Storage NOT have that Azure Files (with identity-based auth) does?
choices: NTFS-style ACLs on individual files/folders after share access is granted
Azure RBAC data roles
SAS tokens
Account access keys
correctAnswers: NTFS-style ACLs on individual files/folders after share access is granted
explanation: Blob relies on RBAC/SAS/keys; Azure Files adds Windows NTFS ACLs on top of share-level access.

prompt: A compromised admin account deletes a critical container. Which pre-configured feature would have allowed recovery within the retention window?
choices: Soft delete for containers/blobs
Dynamic data masking
A minimum TLS version
Infrastructure double encryption
correctAnswers: Soft delete for containers/blobs
explanation: Soft delete makes deleted containers/blobs recoverable during the retention window. The others don't address deletion recovery.

prompt: Why should account access keys, if used at all, be stored in Key Vault and rotated regularly?
choices: They grant full, root-like access to the account and are high-value if leaked
They only work when stored in Key Vault
Rotation is required for encryption at rest to function
Key Vault is the only place a SAS can be generated
correctAnswers: They grant full, root-like access to the account and are high-value if leaked
explanation: Account keys are powerful, root-equivalent credentials; vaulting and rotating them limits exposure if compromised.

prompt: Which recovery feature preserves the state of a blob BEFORE it is overwritten by a new upload?
choices: Versioning
Soft delete
Immutable storage
Point-in-time restore only
correctAnswers: Versioning
explanation: Versioning captures the prior version on each overwrite. Soft delete recovers deletions; immutability blocks changes entirely.

prompt: Which choice best forces all applications to use identity-based access to a storage account?
choices: Disable shared-key authorization and assign Azure RBAC data roles
Rotate the account keys daily
Issue long-lived SAS tokens to every app
Enable anonymous read for internal apps
correctAnswers: Disable shared-key authorization and assign Azure RBAC data roles
explanation: Disabling shared-key access and using RBAC data roles enforces Entra-based access for all apps.

prompt: A user-delegation SAS is considered more secure than an account-key SAS because it is:
choices: Signed with Microsoft Entra credentials rather than the account key
Valid indefinitely
Encrypted at rest in Key Vault
Scoped to the whole subscription
correctAnswers: Signed with Microsoft Entra credentials rather than the account key
explanation: A user-delegation SAS is signed with Entra credentials, so it doesn't depend on (or expose) the powerful account key.

prompt: For a lift-and-shift Windows file server to Azure Files with existing NTFS permissions and no domain controller in Azure, which identity source should you enable?
choices: Microsoft Entra Kerberos
On-premises AD DS with a DC deployed in Azure
SAS tokens per user
Azure RBAC data roles only
correctAnswers: Microsoft Entra Kerberos
explanation: Entra Kerberos is the cloud-only identity source needing no domain controller, while still supporting NTFS ACLs on folders.

prompt: Which two statements about Azure Storage data-protection features are correct? (Choose two.)
choices: Soft delete undoes accidental deletion within a retention window
Versioning preserves the history of blob overwrites
Immutable storage can be bypassed by an owner during retention
BYOK removes the need for encryption at rest
correctAnswers: Soft delete undoes accidental deletion within a retention window
Versioning preserves the history of blob overwrites
explanation: Immutable storage specifically cannot be bypassed during retention, and BYOK changes key ownership, not whether encryption exists.

prompt: Which two statements about Azure Files identity-based authentication are correct? (Choose two.)
choices: Only one identity source can be enabled per storage account
Microsoft Entra Kerberos requires no on-premises domain controller
You can mix AD DS and Entra Kerberos across shares of the same account
NTFS ACLs stop applying once identity-based auth is enabled
correctAnswers: Only one identity source can be enabled per storage account
Microsoft Entra Kerberos requires no on-premises domain controller
explanation: One source per account is a hard limit, and Entra Kerberos is the DC-free option; NTFS ACLs remain fully relevant.

prompt: Which two are ways to authorize access to a storage account? (Choose two.)
choices: Microsoft Entra ID with Azure RBAC data roles
Shared access signatures (SAS)
A user-defined route
A network security group
correctAnswers: Microsoft Entra ID with Azure RBAC data roles
Shared access signatures (SAS)
explanation: Entra RBAC and SAS (and shared keys) authorize access; UDRs and NSGs are network controls, not authorization methods.

prompt: Referring to requirement 3 (migrate a Windows file server to Azure Files, keep NTFS permissions, no domain controller in Azure), which configuration fits?
choices: Enable Microsoft Entra Kerberos and apply NTFS permissions on the folders
Enable on-premises AD DS and deploy a DC in Azure
Use SAS tokens for each user's folder
Use Azure RBAC data roles only, without NTFS
correctAnswers: Enable Microsoft Entra Kerberos and apply NTFS permissions on the folders
explanation: Entra Kerberos needs no domain controller and still supports NTFS ACLs on folders.

prompt: Referring to requirement 4 (blob backups protected so even a compromised admin cannot delete or shorten retention during the retention period), which feature applies?
choices: A time-based immutable storage (WORM) policy set to Locked
Soft delete alone
Blob versioning alone
A read-only SAS token
correctAnswers: A time-based immutable storage (WORM) policy set to Locked
explanation: Only a locked WORM policy prevents deletion or retention-shortening during the period, regardless of privilege.

prompt: Northwind wants the strongest, key-free way for its applications (running on Azure VMs) to read from blob storage. What should they adopt?
choices: Managed identities on the VMs with Azure RBAC data roles on the storage account, and disable shared-key access
Distribute the account access key to each application
Generate long-lived account-key SAS tokens per application
Enable anonymous read access on the containers
correctAnswers: Managed identities on the VMs with Azure RBAC data roles on the storage account, and disable shared-key access
explanation: Managed identities + RBAC data roles give credential-free, identity-based access; disabling shared-key access enforces it.

prompt: Order the steps to migrate an on-premises file server to Azure Files without deploying a domain controller in Azure.
reorderItems: Enable Microsoft Entra Kerberos authentication on the storage account
Assign Azure RBAC data roles for share-level access
Apply NTFS permissions on individual folders
explanation: The share and identity source must exist before share-level RBAC is assigned, with fine-grained NTFS permissions applied last.

prompt: Order the steps to make blob backups tamper-proof for a 7-year compliance retention.
reorderItems: Enable a time-based immutability policy on the container
Set the retention period to 7 years
Confirm that deletion attempts are blocked during the retention period
explanation: The policy and its retention period must be set before locking it irreversibly, with verification last.

prompt: Configure this simplified storage account security screen to force identity-based access only, restricted to selected networks.
explanation: Disabling shared-key access forces Entra-based access, secure transfer enforces HTTPS, and selected-networks access matches the 'restricted' requirement.

prompt: Proposed solution: Share the storage account's primary access key with the partner. Does this solution meet the goal?
choices: Yes
No
correctAnswers: No
explanation: The account key grants broad, root-like access to the whole account — the opposite of scoped, read-only, time-limited access.

prompt: Proposed solution: Issue a user-delegation SAS scoped to that blob with read-only rights and an expiry. Does this solution meet the goal?
choices: Yes
No
correctAnswers: Yes
explanation: A user-delegation SAS is scoped, time-limited, and revocable, and it doesn't require sharing the account keys.

prompt: Proposed solution: Make the blob container public for anonymous read access. Does this solution meet the goal?
choices: Yes
No
correctAnswers: No
explanation: This exposes the data to anyone, not just the partner, and isn't time-limited — it fails the scoped-access requirement.

prompt: Which command forces all connections to a storage account to use HTTPS with at least TLS 1.2?
choices: az storage account update --name stg1 --resource-group RG1 --https-only true --min-tls-version TLS1_2
az storage account update --name stg1 --resource-group RG1 --secure-transfer on --tls 1.2
az storage account tls set --name stg1 --minimum 1.2
az storage container update --name docs --https true
correctAnswers: az storage account update --name stg1 --resource-group RG1 --https-only true --min-tls-version TLS1_2
explanation: --https-only and --min-tls-version (format TLS1_2) are the real parameters on `az storage account update`. The other syntaxes don't exist.

prompt: Which command blocks all shared-key authorization on a storage account, forcing Entra-based access?
choices: az storage account update --name stg1 --resource-group RG1 --allow-shared-key-access false
az storage account keys delete --account-name stg1 --all
az storage account update --name stg1 --resource-group RG1 --default-action Deny
az storage account revoke-delegation-keys --name stg1
correctAnswers: az storage account update --name stg1 --resource-group RG1 --allow-shared-key-access false
explanation: --allow-shared-key-access false disables key-based authorization. You can't delete account keys; --default-action Deny is a NETWORK control, not an authorization one; revoke-delegation-keys revokes user-delegation SAS.

prompt: Which command generates a user-delegation SAS (signed with Entra credentials, not the account key) for one blob?
choices: az storage blob generate-sas --account-name stg1 --container-name docs --name file.pdf --permissions r --expiry 2026-08-01 --auth-mode login --as-user
az storage blob generate-sas --account-name stg1 --container-name docs --name file.pdf --permissions r --account-key <key>
az storage account generate-sas --permissions r --services b
az storage blob sign --name file.pdf --entra
correctAnswers: az storage blob generate-sas --account-name stg1 --container-name docs --name file.pdf --permissions r --expiry 2026-08-01 --auth-mode login --as-user
explanation: --auth-mode login --as-user produces the user-delegation SAS. Passing --account-key signs with the account key (account-key SAS); `az storage account generate-sas` is account-level; `blob sign` doesn't exist.

prompt: Which pair of commands sets the storage firewall to deny by default, then allows only Subnet1 of VNet1?
choices: az storage account update --default-action Deny ; az storage account network-rule add --vnet-name VNet1 --subnet Subnet1
az storage account update --public-network-access Disabled ; az storage account network-rule add --subnet Subnet1
az network nsg rule create --destination stg1 --source Subnet1
az storage firewall set --mode Deny --allow VNet1/Subnet1
correctAnswers: az storage account update --default-action Deny ; az storage account network-rule add --vnet-name VNet1 --subnet Subnet1
explanation: Default-action Deny + network-rule add is the selected-networks pattern. public-network-access Disabled would shut the public endpoint entirely, making the subnet rule pointless; NSGs don't govern the storage service side.

prompt: Which command enables blob soft delete with a 14-day retention?
choices: az storage account blob-service-properties update --account-name stg1 --resource-group RG1 --enable-delete-retention true --delete-retention-days 14
az storage account update --name stg1 --soft-delete 14
az storage blob undelete-policy set --days 14
az storage container immutability-policy create --period 14
correctAnswers: az storage account blob-service-properties update --account-name stg1 --resource-group RG1 --enable-delete-retention true --delete-retention-days 14
explanation: Blob soft delete is a blob-service property (--enable-delete-retention). The immutability-policy command configures WORM (a different feature); the other two don't exist.
